content.assessment: As a no-new-policy baseline, this proposal requires no legal authority change and raises no new rights conflicts by definition; its governance implications are those of inaction under demographic pressure operating within the unchanged legal-constitutional framework. The lens finds it legally uncomplicated but governance-inert: it neither exercises nor claims accountability for outcomes, which is itself a governance characteristic worth flagging.
opportunities: Serves a legitimate governance function as an explicit counterfactual baseline, which is analytically useful for evaluating whether active legal interventions produce marginal accountability gains
strengths: Requires no legislative or constitutional action, and therefore carries zero legal-authority risk, since it changes nothing in the codified structure (F-live-legal_and_governance-01) or the constitutional choice guarantee (F-live-legal_and_governance-02)
threats: Passive contraction concentrated in the state sector while church/private schools remain outside Klebelsberg Centre jurisdiction could produce a governance-accountability vacuum where no authority is responsible for the resulting uneven outcomes (F-live-legal_and_governance-07)
weaknesses: Leaves unaddressed the outlier degree of selectivity and the highest-in-EU segregation level, meaning existing legal and governance structures continue producing the same accountability gap regardless of demographic contraction (F-live-legal_and_governance-04, F-live-legal_and_governance-06)

Provides no mechanism to manage the differential legal reach across maintainer types, so church/private growth could proceed unchecked even as state-sector capacity mechanically shrinks (F-live-legal_and_governance-07)
